questions.question: In the standard large-scale request path, where are reads and writes directed once database replication (read/write split) is in place?
options: Writes go to the primary; reads are served from read replicas
Writes go to read replicas; reads are served from the primary
Reads and writes are both spread evenly across all replicas
Writes go to the cache; reads are served from the primary
questions.explanation: Writes land on the single primary and reads are routed to replicas, which is what scales read-heavy workloads. Replicas cannot accept writes, and the cache is not the write target of record.

questions.question: For a read-heavy system whose database is saturated, what is the highest-leverage first scaling move?
options: Add a cache (e.g. Redis) to serve hot reads from memory
Shard the database across many nodes immediately
Add more stateless web servers behind the load balancer
Move to a multi-region active-active deployment
questions.explanation: A cache removes read load before it reaches the database, the highest-leverage move for read-heavy systems. Adding web nodes does nothing for a DB bottleneck, and sharding is the big, hard last step.

questions.question: What does sharding (partitioning rows across nodes by key) primarily scale that read replicas do not?
options: Write throughput and total storage capacity
Read throughput across the fleet
Network bandwidth to the CDN
The cache hit ratio for hot keys
questions.explanation: Sharding is the step that finally scales writes and storage because rows are split across nodes. Replicas only scale reads since every write still hits the single primary.

questions.question: What is the standard pattern for storing and serving a user-uploaded profile video?
options: Put the bytes in object storage, a pointer plus metadata in the DB, and serve via a CDN
Store the video bytes as a BLOB column in the relational database and serve from the app tier
Keep the bytes in Redis and serve them from the cache on every request
Index the bytes in the search engine and query it to retrieve the video
questions.explanation: Large immutable binaries belong in object storage with a pointer/metadata row in the DB, served through a CDN. Putting big binaries in the relational DB bloats backups and the buffer cache; Redis and the search index are the wrong tools.

questions.question: A round trip across the world (e.g. California to Europe) is roughly 150 ms, a floor set by the speed of light that more money cannot buy away.
questions.explanation: A cross-world round trip is about 150 ms, a speed-of-light floor that no amount of money buys you out of. This is exactly why caches, CDNs, and multi-region deployments exist.

questions.question: Data can be cached at many hops between the user and disk. What is the core correctness rule as you push caching further from the source of truth (toward the user)?
options: Data can be staler and invalidation harder, so personalized data needs private/no-store
Data is always fresher because the cache sits physically closer to the user
Invalidation becomes trivial because each layer auto-expires on every write
Shared edge layers may safely cache personalized responses near the user
questions.explanation: The further from the source of truth, the staler data can be and the harder invalidation gets; personalized or sensitive responses must be marked private/no-store so a shared layer never caches them. Caching closer to the user trades freshness for speed, it does not improve freshness.

questions.question: Why is consistent hashing preferred over `node = hash(key) % N` for distributing keys across cache or shard nodes?
options: Changing N under modulo remaps almost every key, while consistent hashing moves only about K/N
Modulo hashing cannot distribute keys evenly, while consistent hashing alone guarantees a perfect split
Consistent hashing eliminates all hash collisions between distinct keys
Consistent hashing makes every node own identical keys, removing replication
questions.explanation: Under modulo, changing N reshuffles nearly all keys, causing a cache-miss storm or full reshuffle; consistent hashing moves only one arc (~K/N). Even load actually comes from virtual nodes, not the ring alone, and collisions are not what it solves.

questions.question: Mapping each physical node to many positions on the ring (virtual nodes) evens out load and, when a node is lost, spreads its keys across all surviving nodes rather than dumping them onto a single neighbor.
questions.explanation: Virtual nodes place each physical node at many ring spots, which balances load and ensures a lost node's keys spread across all survivors instead of overloading one clockwise neighbor.

questions.question: You need to push one-way, server-to-client notification and live-score updates over a single long-lived HTTP connection. Which pattern fits best?
options: Server-Sent Events (SSE)
WebSocket
Short polling
Webhook
questions.explanation: SSE streams events server-to-client over one long-lived HTTP response, ideal for one-way push feeds. WebSocket is full-duplex (overkill for one-way), short polling wastes requests, and a webhook is server-to-server.

questions.question: Why does a multi-node deployment of WebSocket/SSE servers need a pub/sub backplane (e.g. Redis)?
options: Connections are pinned to one node, so an event on node A must reach a client on node B
WebSockets require sticky sessions at the DNS layer, which only a backplane provides
The backplane is required to terminate TLS for each persistent connection
A backplane is the only way to compress the event payloads sent to clients
questions.explanation: Because each persistent connection lives on one node, an event originating on node A won't reach a client attached to node B without a shared pub/sub backplane to fan the event across nodes. TLS termination, compression, and DNS stickiness are unrelated to that fan-out problem.

questions.question: When answering a geohash-based "find nearby" query, why must you also scan the query point's eight neighbor cells rather than only its own cell prefix?
options: Two physically close points can sit just across a cell boundary and share no prefix
Geohash prefixes are not actually sortable, so neighbors are needed to sort the results
Each cell stores only one point, so neighbors are required to find any other matches
Neighbor cells are needed to convert the geohash back into latitude and longitude
questions.explanation: Real-world proximity can straddle a cell boundary where the keys diverge, so a point just across the border shares no prefix; including the eight neighbor cells catches those. Geohash prefixes are sortable and cells hold many points, so the other options are false.

questions.question: Which statements correctly contrast Lambda and Kappa architectures? Select all that apply.
options: Lambda runs an accurate batch layer alongside a fast, approximate speed layer and merges them in a serving layer
Kappa uses a single replayable stream and reprocesses by replaying the log from the start
A drawback of Lambda is maintaining two separate code paths
Kappa requires maintaining two code paths, one batch and one stream, just like Lambda
MapReduce and Spark are stream engines, while Flink and Kafka Streams are batch engines
questions.explanation: Lambda merges a batch and a speed layer (two code paths, its main drawback); Kappa keeps one replayable stream and reprocesses by replaying. Kappa's whole point is avoiding the second code path, and MapReduce/Spark are batch while Flink/Kafka Streams are stream, so the last two are wrong.

questions.question: Decoupling slow work with a message queue and async workers buys load leveling, but at what cost? Select all that apply.
options: Eventual consistency — the result isn't ready when the call returns
At-least-once delivery, so workers must be idempotent
Strong, immediate consistency of the worker's result for the caller
Guaranteed exactly-once delivery with no idempotency concerns
questions.explanation: Queues introduce eventual consistency (the work finishes after the 202 response) and typically at-least-once delivery, which forces workers to be idempotent. The queue does not give immediate strong consistency, and exactly-once is not guaranteed by the broker.

questions.question: Past three nines, where does most downtime come from, and what mainly buys the higher nines?
options: Deploys, config errors, and human mistakes — bought with automation, canary/rollback, observability
Disk and CPU hardware failures — bought mainly by adding more redundant servers
Network packet loss in transit — bought mainly by switching to a faster CDN
DNS propagation delays at the edge — bought mainly by setting shorter record TTLs
questions.explanation: Beyond three nines downtime is dominated by deploys, config changes, and human error, so progress comes from automation, canary/rollback, and observability rather than more hardware. The expensive nines are operational, not architectural.

questions.question: Two replicas each 99% available are claimed to give `1 − 0.01² = 99.99%` combined. What is the key caveat that makes this number optimistic in practice?
options: It holds only if failures are truly independent, but shared dependencies cause correlated outages
It holds only when both replicas are placed in the same availability zone
It assumes the replicas are read-only, which actually lowers real availability
It double-counts the primary, so the real combined figure is closer to 99%
questions.explanation: Redundancy multiplies failure probabilities only when failures are independent; shared dependencies and correlated outages break that assumption, so 99.99% is optimistic. The formula itself is correct under independence, not invalidated by zones, read-only status, or double-counting.

questions.question: How does Change Data Capture (CDC) keep a search index or cache in sync with the database, and what problem does it avoid?
options: It tails the database write-ahead log, emitting an event per row change, avoiding dual-writes
It periodically runs a full table scan and diffs it against the index, avoiding a write-ahead log
It writes synchronously to both DB and index in one distributed transaction, avoiding eventual consistency
It has the application publish events before it commits, avoiding the write-ahead log entirely
questions.explanation: CDC tails the DB's write-ahead log and emits an event per row change, the log-based alternative to dual-writes from the application. It is not a full-scan diff, not a synchronous distributed transaction, and the application-level before-commit publish describes the outbox pattern, not CDC.

questions.question: Given this Bloom filter check, which statement about its return values is correct?
options: A `False` is always correct (no false negatives); a `True` may be a false positive
A `True` is always correct, but a `False` can be a spurious false negative
Both `True` and `False` can be wrong, so the result is only a rough hint
Neither result is ever wrong because the hash functions are deterministic
questions.explanation: If any bit is 0 the item was definitely never added, so `False` is always correct (no false negatives). When all bits are 1 the item is only probably present, because bits shared across items cause false positives, so `True` can be wrong.

questions.question: A user updates their profile (write to the primary) and the page immediately re-reads it from a read replica, but sees the old value. What is the root cause?
options: Asynchronous replication lag means a read right after a write can be stale (read-your-own-writes)
The replica rejected the write because replicas are read-only, so the new value never saved
The cache returned a stale entry because TTLs do not apply to profile records
Consistent hashing routed the read to the wrong shard and lost the write
questions.explanation: Replicas are updated by asynchronous replication, so a read issued immediately after a write can hit a replica that has not yet applied the change — the classic read-your-own-writes staleness. The write did succeed on the primary; this is lag, not a rejected write, a cache TTL, or a shard-routing error.
